title: The Route Of All Evil

jokes.text: Ah, beer. So many choices. And it makes so little difference.
jokes.setup: Character contemplating beer selection with apparent seriousness
jokes.punchline: The nihilistic conclusion that choice is meaningless
jokes.explanation: Classic Fry-style philosophical observation delivered deadpan. Great economy and character voice.

jokes.text: How about Lobrau? It has dots on it.
jokes.punchline: Fry's criteria is purely visual and meaningless
jokes.explanation: Perfect Fry logic - completely missing the point. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: Overruled! The choice of champions is Pabst Blue Robot.
jokes.punchline: Robot-themed parody of Pabst Blue Ribbon
jokes.explanation: Solid brand parody that fits Bender's character. Competent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: I can't drink that. The metal shavings make my throat bloody.
jokes.setup: Robot beer sounds appealing to humans
jokes.punchline: Literal metal shavings cause physical harm
jokes.explanation: Dark absurdist humor with visceral imagery. Good setup/payoff structure.

jokes.text: Baby wants a Zima.
jokes.punchline: Bender mocks him with reference to notoriously weak drink
jokes.explanation: Classic Bender mockery. Relies on pop culture reference but fits character.

jokes.text: The kids at the orphanarium used to do it all the time.
jokes.punchline: Portmanteau reveals dark orphanage/aquarium hybrid
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that reveals character backstory. Classic Futurama neologism with dark implications.

jokes.text: Cursed bacteria of Liberia! My own son, suspended from boarding school.
jokes.punchline: Absurdly scientific/geographic oath
jokes.explanation: Perfect Professor oath - scientific and oddly specific. Great character voice.

jokes.text: I cloned you from one of my warts, and I can send you straight back there.
jokes.punchline: Grotesque origin story and impossible threat
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly twisted parental threat that establishes sci-fi logic. Dark and memorable.

jokes.text: What's going on? Is this angry yelling, or busted-hearing-aid yelling?
jokes.punchline: Practical distinction between types of yelling
jokes.explanation: Smart observational humor about elderly confusion. Good structure and economy.

jokes.text: I'm afraid it's both. - What?
jokes.setup: Leela confirms both types of yelling
jokes.punchline: Professor immediately proves the hearing aid point
jokes.explanation: Perfect immediate payoff. The 'What?' instantly proves Leela's point about hearing issues.

jokes.text: Everyone cool your daiquiris.
jokes.setup: Standard 'cool your jets' calming phrase expected
jokes.punchline: Substitution with cocktail reference
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay that fits Hermes' Caribbean background. Competent but unremarkable.

jokes.text: It must be hungry. - Black holes don't need food. - Neither do nerds.
jokes.setup: Scientific discussion about black hole properties
jokes.punchline: Bully applies same logic to insult nerds
jokes.explanation: Clever parallel structure. Bully logic that's actually pretty witty for a school antagonist.

jokes.text: My Manwich!
jokes.punchline: Specific brand name creates absurd specificity
jokes.explanation: Brand name humor that's mildly amusing but not deeply clever. Gets laugh from specificity.

jokes.text: You've compressed our lunches to a singularity... for the last time.
jokes.punchline: Treating scientific impossibility as routine classroom disruption
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist premise - treating physics violations as minor misbehavior. Classic sci-fi comedy.

jokes.text: When I re-solidify, I'm gonna put you in a world of goop.
jokes.setup: Bully making threat while being sucked into black hole
jokes.punchline: Threat altered by current state of matter dissolution
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay on 'world of hurt' modified by sci-fi circumstances. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: I warned you not to use those things!
jokes.setup: Father figure upset about children's actions
jokes.punchline: Treating intelligence/brains as forbidden items
jokes.explanation: Anti-intellectual humor that fits bureaucrat character. Solid structure and delivery.

jokes.text: Well, well, if it isn't my old friends, Stretch Pants, No Pants... and Idiot.
jokes.punchline: Descriptive nicknames based on visual appearance
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment through insults. The escalation from descriptive to generic is well-structured.

jokes.text: We're making beer. I'm the brewery.
jokes.punchline: Robot literally serving as manufacturing equipment
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly efficient joke that establishes the episode's central premise. Perfect Bender logic.

jokes.text: I heard alcohol makes you stupid. - No, I'm... Doesn't.
jokes.setup: Kid making claim about alcohol's effects
jokes.punchline: Fry's response immediately demonstrates the truth of the claim
jokes.explanation: Perfect self-demonstrating joke. Fry's fumbled denial proves the point instantly. Great structure.

jokes.text: Alcohol is very, very bad. For children. But once you turn 21... it becomes very, very good.
jokes.punchline: Arbitrary age cutoff magically transforms substance properties
jokes.explanation: Smart social commentary on arbitrary legal distinctions. Good observational humor.

jokes.text: A device that lets anyone sound exactly like me.
jokes.punchline: Completely narcissistic purpose for technology
jokes.explanation: Perfect Professor vanity. Sets up the voice device gag while revealing character.

jokes.text: Good news, everyone. I'm a horse's butt.
jokes.punchline: Catchphrase followed by self-deprecating insult
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion of familiar catchphrase. The juxtaposition of optimistic opening with insult works well.

jokes.text: I am? That's not good news at all, you little...
jokes.setup: Professor hearing his voice say something insulting
jokes.punchline: Momentary confusion before realizing he's been tricked
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat showing Professor's momentary confusion. Natural response to the voice trick.

jokes.text: There is no target consumer. Only targets.
jokes.punchline: Sinister reframing revealing malevolent intent
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that transforms business jargon into supervillain rhetoric. Perfect economy and dark humor.

jokes.text: Targets that will tremble in fear as their new masters hand down edicts... in my glorious, booming voice!
jokes.setup: Professor explaining his invention's evil purpose
jokes.punchline: Grandiose villain speech that reveals petty vanity about his voice
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that maintains the Professor's narcissistic focus. The vanity about his voice is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Wow! A power stamper.
jokes.punchline: Genuine excitement over mundane bureaucratic tool
jokes.explanation: Mild humor about child's easily impressed nature. Shows the generation gap in technology appreciation.

jokes.text: Now look at all the work I gotta do!
jokes.punchline: Visual implication that the 'work' is fixing the stamper damage the kids caused
jokes.explanation: Visual payoff to the stamper interaction. Works better with animation than transcript alone.

jokes.text: Yeast? You mean I'll have a life form growing inside me?
jokes.punchline: Robot expressing concern about biological process in mechanical terms
jokes.explanation: Good robot/biology confusion. Sets up Bender's pregnancy-like beer brewing arc. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: It's so beautiful.
jokes.setup: Bender contemplating yeast growing inside him
jokes.punchline: Unexpected emotional response to microscopic organisms
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing Bender's unexpected paternal/maternal instincts. Simple but effective.

jokes.text: You must deliver a pizza to Dog Doo 8... a planet at the edge of the universe. Sorry I can't come to say goodbye, but I'm busy inventing useless junk.
jokes.punchline: Self-deprecating honesty about the Professor's work habits
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the voice device and Professor's character flaws. The honesty makes it funnier.

jokes.text: And I smell bad.
jokes.setup: List of reasons Professor can't say goodbye
jokes.punchline: Unnecessary additional personal flaw
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and economy. The unnecessary additional insult feels very Professor-like.

jokes.text: When he got home from the Senate.
jokes.setup: Amy explaining her father would discipline the kids
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to father being a Senator, explaining his absence
jokes.explanation: Good character detail that explains Amy's privileged background. Casual delivery makes it work.

jokes.text: The address was on Dog Doo 8. But the universe ends right after Dog Doo 7.
jokes.setup: Explanation of why delivery mission was fake
jokes.punchline: Scatological planet names and universe boundary logic
jokes.explanation: Good sci-fi absurdism with juvenile humor. The universe boundary logic is nicely specific.

jokes.text: Lies! Lies and slander! Accusing gentle Bender of a misdeed? That's the last straw!
jokes.setup: Bender being accused of making bootleg beer
jokes.punchline: Overblown innocent act despite obviously being guilty
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bender overreaction. 'Gentle Bender' is a nice touch that shows his delusion.

jokes.text: Get a job, you lazy kids.
jokes.punchline: Generic parental command delivered with authority
jokes.explanation: Standard parental cliché but fits the moment. Not particularly clever but serves the story.

jokes.text: I guess if you want children beaten, you have to do it yourself.
jokes.punchline: Casual acceptance of child abuse as DIY project
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered with Professor's matter-of-fact tone. The DIY framing makes it absurd rather than genuinely threatening.

jokes.text: Perhaps they've constructed a teddy bear hospital.
jokes.punchline: Overly precious assumption about childish enterprise
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing Hermes' more sentimental side. The specificity of 'teddy bear hospital' is charming.

jokes.text: Who's going to use a delivery service with a kicked sign?
jokes.setup: Bender critiquing the kids' business presentation
jokes.punchline: Practical concern about damaged signage affecting credibility
jokes.explanation: Reasonable business critique that shows Bender's practical side. Visual element works better in animation.

jokes.text: So your delivery company is just a cute, harmless paper route.
jokes.setup: Kids explaining their newspaper delivery contract
jokes.punchline: Dismissive characterization that minimizes their achievement
jokes.explanation: Standard adult condescension toward children's efforts. Sets up later reversal when kids succeed.

jokes.text: What if a paper were to land in a puddle? Civilization as we know it might get splashed.
jokes.setup: Professor listing potential newspaper delivery disasters
jokes.punchline: Absurd escalation ending in pun about splash/civilization impact
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from mundane concern to cosmic consequences. The pun on 'splashed' ties it together perfectly.

jokes.text: We're practically old enough to find the Fox Network infantile.
jokes.setup: Kid complaining about being treated immaturely
jokes.punchline: Self-aware joke about the show's own network
jokes.explanation: Clever meta humor that works on multiple levels. Self-deprecating network joke while advancing character motivation.

jokes.text: Voilà! You got a quarter?
jokes.setup: Cubert presenting their 'sturdy interstellar delivery craft'
jokes.punchline: Reveal that it's apparently a coin-operated kiddie ride
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with perfect economy. The contrast between 'Voilà!' and needing a quarter is beautifully deflating.

jokes.text: Man! The ad said to allow four to six seconds for delivery. More like seven.
jokes.punchline: Treating one-second delay as serious customer service failure
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery of absurd complaint. The specificity and seriousness make it very funny.

jokes.text: I'm really starting to swell up with beer. I must look ridiculous.
jokes.punchline: Self-conscious vanity despite being a robot carrying beer
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bender's vanity. The pregnancy parallel works well for physical comedy.

jokes.text: No. You have a healthy glow.
jokes.punchline: Supportive response treating robot like pregnant woman
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing Leela's supportive nature. The pregnancy parallel is well-maintained.

jokes.text: I was thinking Benderbrau if it's an ale... Botwiser if it's a lager. I hope it's a lager. I can take it to a ball game.
jokes.setup: Bender considering names for his beer
jokes.punchline: Brand puns followed by innocent desire for baseball attendance
jokes.explanation: Decent brand puns with nice character moment. Bender's innocent baseball aspiration is endearing.

jokes.text: There's a crack in the hull here. That could cause explosive decompression. Put a sticker on it.
jokes.setup: Serious safety concern about spacecraft integrity
jokes.punchline: Catastrophically inadequate solution proposed matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection from serious problem to absurd solution. Perfect deadpan delivery of dangerous incompetence.

jokes.text: Remember we used to call you that, huh? Tinkler.
jokes.punchline: Embarrassing bathroom-related nickname shared publicly
jokes.explanation: Good cringe humor about embarrassing parental oversharing. The repetition adds to the awkwardness.

jokes.text: Only the Sunday edition can do's that.
jokes.setup: Comment that newspapers won't deliver themselves
jokes.punchline: Exception for Sunday papers having autonomous delivery capability
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic that feels perfectly Futurama. The casual delivery of impossible information is perfectly Professor.

jokes.text: That's a big number, 50. - Yeah. If you're an idiot.
jokes.setup: Professor making obvious statement about number size
jokes.punchline: Cubert's brutal dismissal of his father's intelligence
jokes.explanation: Perfect father-son dynamic. Cubert's savage dismissal is both harsh and economical. Great structure.

jokes.text: Take evasive action! It's closing in. You can't avoid it. It's a cheek-seeker.
jokes.punchline: Applied to avoiding parental affection (heat-seeker missile pun)
jokes.explanation: Brilliant pun that works on multiple levels. Military terminology applied to parental affection is perfectly absurd and well-executed.

jokes.text: Business is down, so I filed papers to have you all reclassified as slaves.
jokes.punchline: Casual bureaucratic approach to human rights violation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hermes bureaucratic evil. The matter-of-fact delivery of something horrific is expertly done. Great character integration.

jokes.text: Need some penny rolls for your profits?
jokes.setup: Bender continuing to mock the kids' business
jokes.punchline: Assumption that their profits are so small they need coin wrappers
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of Bender's condescension. The penny rolls detail is specific and belittling.

jokes.text: Aren't you impressed now, Pops? Aren't you? Aren't you?
jokes.punchline: Desperate repeated pleading for paternal approval
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing desperate need for approval. The repetition makes it both funny and pathetic.

jokes.text: In a small way, yes.
jokes.punchline: Minimal, grudging acknowledgment
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan response that completely deflates Cubert's moment. The understatement is brutally funny and very Professor.

jokes.text: Dwight Lightning.
jokes.punchline: Applying dramatic military-style codenames to business tactics
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing kids' dramatic approach to business. The military naming convention fits their personalities.

jokes.text: I'm burning to death! You know how much an apartment that big would cost on the sun?
jokes.setup: Reality TV show set on the sun
jokes.punchline: Real estate concern amid life-threatening situation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist premise with perfect priorities inversion. Real estate concern during immolation is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Naturally, we're humiliated. That's why we need you, our loyal crew... to make Planet Express 800% more profitable.
jokes.setup: Professor admitting humiliation at sons' success
jokes.punchline: Arbitrary and impossible percentage increase demand
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor with the specific 800% figure being arbitrarily precise yet impossible.

jokes.text: We don't work for you anymore! - What? - Dwight and Cubert made us an offer. - We're paperboys now.
jokes.punchline: Revelation that they've taken children's paper route jobs
jokes.explanation: Good role reversal with adults taking traditionally kid jobs. The escalation and timing work well.

jokes.text: I don't even know who this guy is. - I'm Scruffy, the janitor.
jokes.setup: Hermes not recognizing his own employee
jokes.punchline: Scruffy's matter-of-fact self-introduction
jokes.explanation: Classic Scruffy moment. His deadpan self-introduction after being ignored is perfectly in character and always funny.

jokes.text: I'm on break.
jokes.setup: Scruffy being asked to help save the company
jokes.punchline: Prioritizing union break time over crisis
jokes.explanation: Perfect Scruffy moment. The timing and delivery are flawless. Maximum comedy with minimum words and perfect character consistency.

jokes.text: Sweet guinea pig of Winnipeg! They've taken over our company.
jokes.punchline: Absurd geographical/zoological oath
jokes.explanation: Another great Professor oath. 'Guinea pig of Winnipeg' is wonderfully specific and absurd. Good callback to his swearing pattern.

jokes.text: Tax dodge, nothing! You take one nap in a ditch at the park... and they start declaring you this and that.
jokes.setup: Professor defending his tax dodge claim
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation of homeless-like behavior treated as minor inconvenience
jokes.explanation: Perfect Professor logic. The casual treatment of sleeping in ditches as bureaucratic overreach is brilliantly absurd.

jokes.text: You rotten kids! - Will you be hiring? - No. - You rotten kids!
jokes.punchline: Immediate attempt to get job followed by continued insults when rejected
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency showing Bender's opportunism and immediate return to hostility when rejected. Nice structure.

jokes.text: They'll make the ship go faster. - And what's your scientific basis for thinking that? - I'm 12.
jokes.setup: Adult asking for scientific reasoning behind modification
jokes.punchline: Age as complete scientific justification
jokes.explanation: Perfect child logic that actually makes sense in its own way. The economy and structure are excellent. Great setup/payoff.

jokes.text: Push, Bender! Push!
jokes.setup: Bender's beer brewing process resembling childbirth
jokes.punchline: Leela coaching him like a midwife
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of the pregnancy metaphor. Leela's supportive coaching maintains the birthing parallel effectively.

jokes.text: Oh, it feels like it's trying to push a waterbed out of me.
jokes.setup: Bender describing the sensation of beer brewing
jokes.punchline: Bizarre comparison combining pregnancy imagery with random furniture
jokes.explanation: Creative and weird comparison that feels very Bender. The waterbed imagery is unexpected and viscerally funny.

jokes.text: It's an ale. Five gallons, six ounces.
jokes.punchline: Reporting beer type and quantity like a baby's vital statistics
jokes.explanation: Perfect birth announcement parody. The specific measurements make it feel authentic while being absurd.

jokes.text: This is a delivery company, not a delivery room.
jokes.setup: Cubert arriving to see the beer 'birth' scene
jokes.punchline: Pun on delivery company vs delivery room
jokes.explanation: Solid pun with great economy that works well with the birth parody scene setup.

jokes.text: Hey, wait a second. How did you deliver a million papers in one hour?
jokes.setup: Bender finally asking the logical question
jokes.punchline: Pointing out the obvious impossibility everyone missed
jokes.explanation: Good story logic moment where someone finally asks the obvious question. Sets up the revelation of their fraud.

jokes.text: Don't you sweet-talk me... you wrinkly old tube sock.
jokes.punchline: Amy's harsh rejection with oddly specific insult
jokes.explanation: Good Amy moment with creative insult. 'Wrinkly old tube sock' is specific and visual enough to be memorable.

jokes.text: Now, you should be happy that they became successes... instead of following in your food stamps.
jokes.punchline: Malapropism replacing 'footsteps' with welfare reference
jokes.explanation: Excellent malapropism that changes the meaning completely while maintaining sentence structure. Classic Amy verbal fumble.

jokes.text: We couldn't handle them. We dumped the extras in a crater on the moon.
jokes.setup: Kids confessing to their business failure
jokes.punchline: Casual admission of massive environmental crime
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of their fraud. The casual moon dumping feels very Futurama in its environmental disregard.

jokes.text: You ruined us with sleazy business practices... and a complete disregard for human decency. - Of course we're proud of you.
jokes.punchline: Expressing pride because of, not despite, these qualities
jokes.explanation: Perfect inversion of parental values. The setup lists objectively bad qualities, making the pride absurdly inappropriate but character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Good thing I had this net installed for catching giraffes.
jokes.punchline: Explanation that it was designed for completely different impossible purpose
jokes.explanation: Classic Professor absurdity. The casual mention of giraffe-catching as normal home preparation is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Can I use the gun, Dad? - What kind of father would I be if I said no?
jokes.punchline: Father treating weapon access as good parenting
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about parenting philosophy. The inversion of responsible parenting into weapon sharing is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Run away! That bully, Brett Blob, lives there. He's ugly, mean, stupid, stink mucus!
jokes.punchline: Escalating insults ending in nonsensical 'stink mucus'
jokes.explanation: Good escalating child insults. 'Stink mucus' is appropriately gross and nonsensical for a kid's vocabulary.

jokes.text: Oh, heavens, no! We apologize with nice, cheap words.
jokes.setup: Question about paying for broken window
jokes.punchline: Honest admission of preferring insincere apologies to money
jokes.explanation: Perfect Professor cheapness. The phrase 'nice, cheap words' brilliantly captures his philosophy of minimal effort solutions.

jokes.text: I thought I heard the doorbell. But I see it was the 'dork bell.'
jokes.punchline: Doorbell/dork bell pun to insult visitors
jokes.explanation: Standard bully wordplay. Not particularly clever but fits the character and situation adequately.

jokes.text: You shove your apology into the bottom of your one-way digestive system.
jokes.punchline: Pseudo-scientific way of saying 'shove it up your ass'
jokes.explanation: Creative euphemism that's more elaborate than necessary. The scientific terminology makes the insult both funnier and more character-specific.

jokes.text: Tell you what. I'll accept their apology when they kiss my ass! Which I don't have.
jokes.setup: Standard impossible demand for apology acceptance
jokes.punchline: Reveal that the demand is literally impossible due to anatomy
jokes.explanation: Brilliant setup and payoff. The standard angry parent demand is undercut by the biological impossibility, making it both absurd and logical.

jokes.text: You are the bravest dads in the entire trauma center.
jokes.punchline: Scope limited to current location of injured people
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about context-limited praise. The specificity of 'trauma center' makes the compliment less impressive.

jokes.text: If I could feel anything right now, it would be pride. - I was wrong! I can still feel pain!
jokes.punchline: Immediate contradiction when experiencing different sensation
jokes.explanation: Perfect immediate contradiction. The setup establishes numbness, then immediately disproves it with pain, creating great comedic timing.

jokes.text: They're bringing in computers to improve productivity... and, well, that's no excuse for how I acted.
jokes.setup: Mr. Blob explaining his workplace stress
jokes.punchline: Honest self-reflection about inappropriate behavior
jokes.explanation: Good character development moment showing genuine remorse. The workplace automation anxiety feels realistic and relatable.

jokes.text: Hey, chumps. I heard you were on the ass end of an ass-kicking.
jokes.punchline: Double pun on 'ass end' of situation and literal ass-kicking
jokes.explanation: Good double entendre that works literally and figuratively. Classic Bender insensitivity.

jokes.text: Man or blob, it's what's inside that counts.
jokes.punchline: Species-inclusive version of common saying adapted to character
jokes.explanation: Nice adaptation of familiar saying to include the blob character. Works as both humor and genuine sentiment for the episode's resolution.

tmdb_overview: Farnsworth's clone Cubert teams up with Hermes' son Dwight to launch a newspaper delivery business. Farnsworth and Hermes scoff at the kids' efforts - until the delivery boys accumulate enough capital to buy out Planet Express. Meanwhile, Fry and Leela use Bender to brew their own homemade beer.
